name: Eagle Crags
short_description: BLM public land near San Bernardino offering accessible quartz and jasper collecting on gentle slopes. No permits required for personal use.
description:
Eagle Crags is a popular BLM rockhounding site in San Bernardino County situated at nearly 4000 feet elevation with gentle slopes that make it approachable for collectors of all experience levels. The area sits within the Mojave Desert province where Tertiary volcanic and sedimentary formations have created abundant opportunities to find quartz, jasper, agate, and chalcedony specimens. The gentle terrain and proximity to San Bernardino make this an ideal day-trip destination for both beginners and experienced rockhounds.

The geology here reflects the complex history of the Basin and Range province. Silica-rich fluids percolating through fractured granitic and volcanic host rocks during the Tertiary created the colorful quartz and chalcedony deposits found throughout the area. Jasper and agate nodules are commonly discovered in weathered outcrops and wash gravels, with colors ranging from deep reds and browns to translucent whites and grays.

Visitors should expect to walk moderate distances from parking areas to productive collecting zones. The gentle slopes allow for scanning hillsides and creek beds without extreme exertion. Many collectors focus on wash gravels where erosion naturally concentrates specimens. The site remains accessible year-round due to low elevation, though late fall through early spring offers the most comfortable collecting conditions.

Respect BLM regulations and collect responsibly. The 25-pound daily limit ensures sustainable use of this public resource. Leave behind excess material and report any signs of illegal mining activity to local BLM rangers.
history: Eagle Crags sits within the Mojave Desert's Basin and Range geology, where Tertiary-age volcanic activity and hydrothermal circulation deposited silica-rich fluids that crystallized into the quartz, agate, and jasper found here today. This area has been a known rockhounding destination for decades, with informal collecting trails established by local enthusiasts.
tips: Focus your search on dry washes and erosional gullies where water has concentrated lighter specimens over time - jasper and agate gravels collect naturally in these channels. Bring a hand lens to spot the subtle color banding in agate nodules that distinguishes them from plain quartz. Look for slightly darker, reddish specimens partially buried in sandy deposits along hillside slopes, as these often indicate higher-quality material. A small classifier or screen helps quickly sort promising material from overburden.
terrain_notes: Elevation 3996 feet with gentle slopes throughout the area. Terrain is open scrubland with scattered desert vegetation. Footing is generally firm sandy soil with scattered small rocks. Walking distance from parking areas to productive zones typically 0.5 to 2 miles depending on water availability and seasonal conditions. No major elevation gain required.
written_directions: From San Bernardino, take I-15 north approximately 20 miles to the Victorville exit. Head east on CA-18 for roughly 15 miles toward the foothills. Watch for marked BLM access roads heading north off CA-18; Eagle Crags can be accessed via several dirt pullouts along these secondary roads. The final approach is typically on well-graded dirt roads passable by passenger vehicles under normal conditions. Obtain current access information from the BLM Barstow Field Office before visiting.
road_conditions: Well-maintained dirt roads, generally passable year-round by standard vehicles. Some washboard conditions possible after rain.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and parking areas along access roads. No developed parking lot. Space for 3-6 vehicles at most pullouts.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Pack out all waste. Respect any temporary closures or fire restrictions posted by BLM.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection allowed
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Pack out all waste and excess material
Respect posted closures and fire restrictions
No overnight camping without BLM permit
nearest_services: San Bernardino, approximately 25 miles southwest. Full services including gas, food, water, and supplies available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers and families. Dirt road to parking areas navigable by standard vehicles. No wheelchair access to collecting areas due to uneven sandy terrain, but parking locations can be selected to minimize walking distance. Sturdy hiking boots recommended for comfortable footing in sandy soil.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Eagle Crags near San Bernardino?
faq.answer: Eagle Crags is known for quartz, jasper, agate, and chalcedony. The site yields colorful jasper specimens in reds and browns, translucent quartz points, and banded agate nodules. Most collectors focus on wash gravels and hillside slopes where erosion naturally concentrates these silica-based minerals.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Eagle Crags California?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Eagle Crags, as it is BLM public land. You only need to follow the personal use regulations: collect no more than 25 lbs per day, do not use motorized equipment, and do not engage in commercial collection.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound Eagle Crags?
faq.answer: The best season is October through April when temperatures are mild and collecting is most comfortable. Although the site is accessible year-round due to its 4000-foot elevation in the Mojave Desert, summer heat and occasional flash flood risk in washes make cooler months safer and more pleasant.

faq.question: How far is Eagle Crags rockhounding site from San Bernardino?
faq.answer: Eagle Crags is approximately 25 miles northeast of San Bernardino. From downtown San Bernardino, take I-15 north, then CA-18 east toward the foothills where BLM access roads branch north to the collecting areas. The drive takes roughly one hour depending on traffic and final road conditions.

faq.question: Is Eagle Crags rockhounding site beginner friendly and good for families?
faq.answer: Yes, Eagle Crags is ideal for beginners and families. The gentle slopes, accessible dirt roads, and easy walking distances make it suitable for children ages 6 and up. No special equipment or permits are needed, and the site offers straightforward jasper and quartz collecting in an open landscape.

faq.question: What tools should I bring for rockhounding quartz and jasper at Eagle Crags?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand lens to identify banding in agate and distinguish quality specimens, a small classifier or screen to sort material, a sturdy digging tool like a geologist's hammer or pry bar, a collection bag, and plenty of water. Wear sturdy boots for sandy terrain and bring sunscreen, as the exposed desert offers little shade.
